content:
# Exodus Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~32r–32v (PDF pp. 62–63); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 42–43 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_002_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_002_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** A man from the tribe of Levi married a woman of the same tribe.

**2** She became pregnant and gave birth to a son. When she saw how beautiful he was, she hid him for three months.

**3** But when she could hide him no longer, she got a papyrus basket, waterproofed it with tar and pitch, placed the child inside, and set it among the reeds along the bank of the Nile.

**4** His sister stood watch at a distance to see what would happen to him.

**5** Then Pharaoh's daughter came down to bathe in the Nile while her attendants walked along the riverbank. She spotted the basket among the reeds and sent her slave-girl to get it.

**6** She opened it and saw the child — a baby boy, crying. Her heart went out to him. "This must be one of the Hebrew children," she said.

**7** Then the boy's sister stepped forward and said to Pharaoh's daughter, "Shall I go and find a Hebrew woman to nurse the child for you?"

**8** "Yes, go," Pharaoh's daughter told her. So the girl went and brought the child's own mother.

**9** Pharaoh's daughter said to her, "Take this child and nurse him for me, and I will pay you." So the woman took the child and nursed him.

**10** When the boy was old enough, she brought him to Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son. She named him Moses, saying, "Because I drew him out of the water."

**11** Years later, after Moses had grown up, he went out to where his own people were and witnessed their forced labor. He saw an Egyptian beating a Hebrew — one of his own brothers.

**12** He looked in every direction, and when he saw that no one was watching, he struck the Egyptian dead and buried him in the sand.

**13** The next day he went out again and found two Hebrew men fighting. He said to the one in the wrong, "Why are you hitting your fellow Hebrew?"

**14** The man shot back, "Who made you a ruler and judge over us? Are you going to kill me the way you killed the Egyptian?" Moses was terrified and thought, "The thing I did — it's been found out."

**15** When Pharaoh heard about it, he tried to have Moses killed. But Moses fled from Pharaoh and settled in the land of Midian. He sat down beside a well.

**16** Now the priest of Midian had seven daughters. They came to draw water and fill the troughs to water their father's flock.

**17** But shepherds showed up and drove them away. So Moses stood up, came to their rescue, and watered their flock.

**18** When they returned to their father Reuel, he asked, "How did you get back so quickly today?"

**19** They said, "An Egyptian rescued us from the shepherds. He even drew water for us and watered the flock."

**20** "So where is he?" he asked his daughters. "Why did you leave the man behind? Invite him to eat with us!"

**21** Moses agreed to stay with the man, and Reuel gave his daughter Zipporah to Moses in marriage.

**22** She gave birth to a son, and Moses named him Gershom, saying, "I have been a stranger in a foreign land."

**23** A long time passed. The king of Egypt died. And the Israelites groaned under their slavery and cried out — and their cry for help rose up to God from the depths of their bondage.

**24** God heard their groaning. God remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob.

**25** God saw the people of Israel. And God knew.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Manuscript Observations
Both the Leningrad Codex (folio ~32r-32v) and Codex Vaticanus (pp. 42-43) preserve this chapter without significant textual variation. The Leningrad Codex shows the standard Masoretic paragraph divisions (setumah markers between sections). The Vaticanus LXX column text is consistent with the MT for narrative content, with minor expansions typical of the Septuagint translation tradition (e.g., LXX specifies details in the bathing scene). No variants alter the meaning of the passage.

### Key Dynamic Choices

**v.2 — "beautiful" for Hebrew *tov*:** The Hebrew says she saw him and he was *tov* — "good." This is the same word God uses in Genesis 1 when he sees that creation is "good." The echo is intentional: this child is creation-good, life-good. The LXX uses *asteion* ("fine, beautiful"). I render "beautiful" because English "good" has lost its force. But the Genesis resonance matters — the narrator is signaling that this birth is an act of counter-creation against Pharaoh's death decree.

**v.3 — "basket" for Hebrew *tevah*:** This word appears in only two places in the entire Hebrew Bible: here, and for Noah's ark (Genesis 6-9). The narrator deliberately chose *tevah* instead of the common word for basket. Both are vessels of rescue floating on threatening waters. Both save a remnant through which God will act. I use "basket" because "ark" would confuse modern readers, but the Hebrew audience heard the echo immediately — this baby is being placed in an ark.

**v.3 — "waterproofed it with tar and pitch" for *ba-chemar u-va-zafet*:** The Hebrew specifies bitumen (*chemar*) and pitch (*zafet*). The same waterproofing language as Noah's ark (Genesis 6:14 uses *kofer*, a related term). The mother is ark-building.

**v.6 — "Her heart went out to him" for *va-tachmol alav*:** The Hebrew *chamol* means to have compassion, to spare. It is stronger than mere pity — it implies an emotional bond that compels protective action. "Her heart went out to him" captures the involuntary, visceral quality of the response. Pharaoh's own daughter undoes Pharaoh's decree through instinctive compassion.

**v.10 — "Moses" (*Mosheh*) and "drew out" (*mashah*):** The name explanation is a Hebrew wordplay. An Egyptian princess names a child with a Hebrew pun — she says *ki min ha-mayim meshitihu*, "because from the water I drew-him-out." The name could also have Egyptian etymology (*mes/mose* = "born of," as in Thutmose, Rameses). The narrator lets the Hebrew wordplay carry the meaning: Moses is "the one drawn out" — and will become the one who draws others out.

**v.12 — "struck the Egyptian dead":** The Hebrew *va-yakh et ha-mitsri* uses the same verb (*nakah*) that will later describe God's striking of Egypt. I make the killing explicit ("struck...dead") because the Hebrew is direct and the narrative does not euphemize or moralize. The text simply reports what Moses did.

**v.14 — "Who made you a ruler and judge over us?":** This challenge will echo forward — Stephen quotes this verse in Acts 7:27-28, and it becomes a lens for understanding Israel's repeated rejection of its deliverers. The Hebrew *ish sar ve-shofet* — "a man, a prince, and a judge" — is dripping with contempt.

**v.22 — Gershom (*Gershom*):** From *ger* ("stranger, sojourner") + *sham* ("there"). Moses names his son "Stranger-There" — an identity marker of displacement. He is a Hebrew raised Egyptian, now living as a foreigner in Midian. The name carries the ache of exile.

**vv.23-25 — The four divine verbs:** The chapter's climax is one of the most concentrated theological statements in the Torah. Four verbs hammer in sequence: God **heard** (*va-yishma*) their groaning. God **remembered** (*va-yizkor*) his covenant. God **saw** (*va-yar*) the Israelites. God **knew** (*va-yeda*). The Masoretic text leaves the last verb without an object — *va-yeda Elohim*, "and God knew." Knew what? The text does not say. This is deliberate. The open ending is powerful: God knew — everything. Their suffering, their need, their future, the full weight of it. The LXX adds *kai egnosthe autois* ("and he was made known to them"), which fills in the gap but loses the force of the Hebrew silence. I follow the MT and leave it open: "And God knew."

**v.18 — "Reuel" vs. "Jethro":** The Leningrad Codex reads *Re'u'el* here. The same figure is called *Yitro* (Jethro) in Exodus 3:1 and 18:1. Various explanations exist (different names, different traditions, title vs. name). I follow the manuscript as it reads in each passage without harmonizing.

**Structural note:** This chapter moves through three geographic phases: Egypt (vv.1-14), flight/Midian (vv.15-22), and heaven's response (vv.23-25). Each phase features water — the Nile as threat and rescue, the well as meeting place, and the implicit waters of suffering from which God will draw Israel out. Moses' life foreshadows Israel's journey: threatened, rescued through water, exiled, and ultimately called.

--- PNEUMA
